queries.query: I keep hitting wrong notes in passages I've already learned. How do I fix this?
queries.notes: Core wrong-note remediation: slow correct repetition, blind practice, and not ingraining errors directly answer this.

queries.query: How can I practice big leaps across the keyboard without missing the notes I'm jumping to?
queries.notes: Blind-practice chunk explicitly describes leaping then freezing to check hand position; Mechanical Approach covers approaching a note from the prior note.

queries.query: How do I help a student learn the sharps and flats in a piece's key signature?

relevant.section_title: “Chunk Out” The Key’s Scale and Measures
Discover the “Why” With a Circle of 5ths Chart

queries.notes: All three key-signature teaching techniques directly target learning a piece's sharps/flats.

queries.query: What's a good way to stop misreading accidentals while sight-reading?

relevant.section_title: “Chunk Out” The Key’s Scale and Measures
Discover the “Why” With a Circle of 5ths Chart

queries.notes: Keyboard Map maps every accidental before playing; the other two reinforce where/why accidentals occur.

queries.query: I get nervous and play a wrong note right at the spot I knew was coming. Any mental tricks?
queries.notes: Psychology chunk is exactly about anticipating and then playing the dreaded wrong note; the others cover focus and recovering from mistakes.

queries.query: How do I count sixteenth notes out loud when I'm reading rhythm?

relevant.section_title: Start with a Simple Counting System

queries.notes: Both counting chunks give explicit '1 e & a' sixteenth-note syllable systems.

queries.query: My triplets always sound uneven. How should I count and feel them?
queries.notes: Triplet Counting chunk directly addresses counting triplets; subdivision-verbalizing is a related general technique.

queries.query: How should I use a metronome when practicing rhythm so it actually helps?

relevant.section_title: Count Aloud and Practice with a Metronome

queries.notes: Two chunks are explicitly about metronome use (anchor vs. crutch, count aloud while playing); Tips covers slow metronome practice.

queries.query: I tend to rush and drag instead of keeping a steady beat. How can I fix my timing?

relevant.section_title: Practice with Other Musicians or Record Yourself

queries.notes: Recording-yourself chunk names rushing/dragging explicitly; metronome and 'feel the beat' chunks are supporting fixes.

queries.query: What's a systematic way to teach rhythm and note values to a young beginner?

relevant.section_title: Book: I Can Read Music by Joanne Martin
Book: Rhythmic Training by Robert Starer
Start with a Simple Counting System

queries.notes: Note Values lays out the beginner progression; the two book chunks and the simple counting system support a structured teaching sequence.

queries.query: I'm making both wrong-note and timing mistakes in one hard passage. How do I practice it accurately?
queries.notes: Spans both skills: don't ingrain wrong notes (pitch) and don't ingrain sloppy rhythm (timing), both via slow accurate practice.

queries.query: What ear-training exercises help with both pitch recognition and rhythm at the same time?
queries.notes: The Ear Training chunk covers framework-based listening for pitch AND meter/beat-division (rhythm); subdivision aural work is a related rhythm-ear skill.

queries.query: How does practicing slowly with a metronome improve both my note accuracy and my timing?
queries.notes: Slow-correct practice serves pitch accuracy; slow metronome practice serves timing; both perspectives are needed to answer.

queries.query: How should I use the sustain pedal to connect notes in a legato passage?
queries.notes: Pedaling technique is not covered anywhere in this pitch/timing corpus; correct retrieval is nothing relevant.

queries.query: How do I memorize a long piece so I can perform it from memory?
queries.notes: Memorization is not addressed; the performance-psychology chunk is about wrong notes, not memory work, so nothing truly answers this.

queries.query: How can I shape dynamics and play a piece more expressively?
queries.notes: Dynamics and musical expression are out of scope for this pitch/timing corpus.

queries.query: What hand-stretching warm-ups prevent injury when playing fast passages?
queries.notes: Touches physical tension/release during fast passages but is about rhythm practice, not warm-ups or injury prevention; no chunk truly answers.

queries.query: How do I choose the best fingering for a difficult run?
queries.notes: Mechanical Approach mentions observing/working out fingerings in service of accuracy, but the corpus has no real fingering-selection guidance.

queries.query: How can I sight-read a brand-new piece fluently on the first read-through?

relevant.section_title: Start with a Simple Counting System

queries.notes: Corpus covers reading rhythm and reading-ahead focus, which touch sight-reading, but none directly teach first-pass sight-reading fluency.

queries.query: What is a key signature and how does it work?

relevant.section_title: Discover the “Why” With a Circle of 5ths Chart

queries.notes: Corpus is prescriptive practice advice, not theory; this chunk explains the 'why' of key signatures but does not define the concept.

queries.query: What is the circle of fifths in music theory?

relevant.section_title: Discover the “Why” With a Circle of 5ths Chart

queries.notes: Mentions using a Circle of 5ths chart as a teaching aid but never defines what the circle of fifths is; theory query expected to pull only weak noise.

queries.query: What are the names of the musical intervals, like a third or a fifth?
queries.notes: Ear Training references intervals (e.g., a fourth) only to argue against interval-by-interval thinking; it does not define interval names.
